items.q: What did Renaissance artists rediscover that medieval art lacked?
items.correct: Linear perspective and naturalistic representation of the human body.
items.why: Students assume medieval art was colourless. It was vivid but flat - the Renaissance breakthrough was mathematical PERSPECTIVE (Brunelleschi) and anatomical realism, not the introduction of colour.

items.q: What is the rule of thirds?
items.correct: A guideline placing key elements along lines or intersections of a 3x3 grid.
items.misconception: A strict mathematical rule that must never be broken.
items.why: Students treat it as inviolable. It's a useful HEURISTIC for engaging compositions - many great works ignore it. Centred compositions can be powerful too; the rule is a starting point, not a law.

items.correct: Strong contrast between light and dark to model three-dimensional form.
items.why: Students think it's about subject matter (night scenes). Chiaroscuro is a TECHNIQUE using value contrast to suggest volume - exemplified by Caravaggio but used since the Renaissance to model form.

items.q: Which artist's infinity nets typify obsessive mark-making?
items.why: Correct answer: Yayoi Kusama. “Cy Twombly” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many screens does an HL Process Portfolio maximum allow?
items.why: Exact answer: 18 screens. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
